title: Garden Arbor Bench with Trellis Sides
summary: A built-in bench and climbing trellis in one structure, anchored on four buried 6x6 posts.
description:
An arbor alone is a frame you walk through. Add a bench between the posts and it becomes a destination — somewhere to actually sit under the vines you're growing up the trellis sides.

The **posts carry everything**, so they're the one part of this build where you don't cut corners: 6x6 stock, buried two feet in concrete. Every other board — the seat slats, the trellis lattice — is just infill between those four posts.

Build the trellis sides before you commit to a permanent finish. Vines take a season or two to fill in, and it's much easier to stain bare wood than wood with a climbing rose already established on it.

tools.note: Or a miter saw for the crosscuts — you'll want the circular saw regardless for notching the posts.
Driving 3" deck screws all day is easier with one.
Cleans out the post notches after the circular saw's kerfs.
Holds the seat slats level while you screw them in solo.

materials.name: Cedar, 6x6, 10 ft
materials.note: Line total for all four posts — large cedar timbers run $50+ each. Don't substitute a smaller post; the notches for the seat and trellis rely on this much material.

materials.name: Cedar, 2x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Line total for all six boards.

materials.name: Cedar, 2x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Line total for all seven boards.

materials.name: Quick-setting concrete
materials.note: One bag per post, minimum. Don't skimp here — the posts carry the whole structure.

cutList.note: Notched at the top per the plan. Buried 24" in concrete, so order stock long enough to lose two feet into the ground.

steps.title: Notch and set the four posts
steps.body:
Cut the four 6x6 posts to length and notch the tops per your layout — this is what the upper rails will seat into. Dig four holes 24" deep, set the posts, brace them plumb, and pour concrete around the base of each one.

Let the concrete cure before putting any load on the posts. Everything else in this build — bench, trellis, all of it — hangs off these four posts, so an out-of-plumb post here is a problem you'll be fighting for the rest of the build.

steps.title: Install the upper side rails
steps.body: Cut the two 2x6 upper rails to length, notch the ends to match the post notches, and screw them into place near the top of the posts on both sides.

steps.title: Add the mid rails and lower cross braces
steps.body: Install the five 2x2 mid rails below the upper rails on each side, then notch the posts for the 2x4 lower cross braces and install those — measuring up from the ground to account for how deep you buried the posts, so the seat ends up at a sittable height.

steps.title: Build and install the seat
steps.body:
Cut the five 2x4 seat support braces and screw them between the posts at seat height. Cut the seven 2x6 slats to length and screw them across the supports for the seat and backrest.

Clamp each slat level before driving screws if you're working alone — a slat that's screwed down slightly twisted is hard to fix after the fact.

steps.title: Build the side trellises
steps.body:
Cut the trellis uprights and lattice pieces per the cut list, then assemble each trellis frame flat on the ground before attaching it to the sides — it's far easier to keep a lattice square on a flat surface than fastening piece by piece in place vertically.

Attach the finished trellis panels to the outside of each side, between the posts.
